articles.title: Japan: Japanese proficiency essential for foreign graduates staying on to work
articles.summary: An increasing number of study abroad destinations beyond the traditional “Big Four” (Australia, Canada, the UK, and the US) are gaining popularity, supported by government and institutional efforts to boost international enrolments and retain high-skilled foreign graduates. Japan is highlighted as one such destination where Japanese language proficiency is essential for foreign graduates seeking to remain and work after completing their studies.
articles.insight:
This news signals growing competition from non-traditional destinations like Japan, where language requirements create a distinct barrier—and potential filter—for international graduates, possibly reshaping student preferences toward countries with lower linguistic entry thresholds or stronger post-study work pathways.  
Admissions teams should monitor enrolment trends in Japan and similar emerging destinations, particularly among students from East and Southeast Asia, while assessing whether their own visa policies, language support, and graduate employment services remain competitive in attracting high-potential applicants.

articles.title: Lula government will require medical students to prove proficiency in Enamed to practice their profession
articles.summary: President Lula signed a provisional measure on June 19, 2026, requiring medical students in Brazil to demonstrate proficiency through the Enamed (National Medical Training Assessment Examination) to practice medicine. This requirement applies to all doctors seeking to enter professional practice in the country.
articles.insight:
This policy may indirectly influence Latin American student demand for health-related degrees abroad, as heightened domestic licensing requirements could prompt some to seek alternative pathways—but evidence of actual enrollment shifts remains speculative and likely lagged.  
Admissions teams should monitor Brazilian and regional student visa application trends, Enamed pass rates, and local media sentiment around medical education quality, while avoiding premature curriculum or marketing adjustments until clearer migration or enrollment signals emerge.

articles.title: Hong Kong parents turn to summer school options in Asia amid soaring airfares
articles.summary: Hong Kong families are increasingly choosing summer schools in Asia instead of traditional destinations like Britain and North America due to sharply rising airfares, which education consultants attribute partly to the war in the Middle East. Applications for British summer schools dropped by 35 per cent this year, according to Samuel Chan Sze-ming, founder of education consultancy Britannia StudyLink.
articles.insight:
Rising airfares—partly linked to Middle East conflict—are prompting Hong Kong families to shift summer study plans toward nearer Asian destinations, suggesting potential short-term pressure on demand for Western summer programmes, though long-term degree enrolment trends remain uncertain.  
Admissions teams should track Hong Kong application data for summer 2025 and early 2026 intakes, while reviewing pricing, travel support offerings, and regional partnerships to assess whether cost-sensitive families are delaying or reconsidering longer-term study plans.
